local_names.romanized_local: Kunyit serbuk (Malay); Manjal thool (Tamil romanized); Haldi powder (Hindi romanized)

region_of_origin: South Asia (India); widely used across Southeast Asia, Middle East, and globally

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 227493005 refers to turmeric as a substance; no specific ground/powdered form code identified. Closest parent match used as the substance-level code is the nearest defensible ancestor in the SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground turmeric is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 3 g), as used in curries, rice dishes, soups, and golden milk preparations.

nutrient_source.title: Ground Turmeric — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ground turmeric (CR ID: F10060036). All core macros and micronutrients reflect HPB-reported values. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select vitamins not reported by HPB; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02043 (Spices, turmeric, ground) for saturated fat and additional micronutrients where available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on turmeric GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Bhatt et al. (2020) peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground turmeric does not have a directly published GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of approximately 15 is estimated based on: (1) turmeric's very high dietary fibre content (22.7 g/100g), (2) low available carbohydrate density per culinary serving (~2 g per teaspoon), (3) curcumin's documented inhibition of alpha-glucosidase and alpha-amylase enzymes reducing postprandial glucose response, and (4) analogy with other dried ground spices (e.g., cinnamon GI ~5, ginger GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 3 g serving = (15 × 1.34 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.3. This is a low-GI, negligible-GL food in culinary quantities.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (27 mg/100g), exceptionally high potassium (2080 mg/100g), and curcumin's vasodilatory and endothelial-protective properties
hypertension.guidance: Ground turmeric is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content is negligible in culinary quantities (~0.8 mg per teaspoon), its potassium-to-sodium ratio is exceptionally favourable for blood pressure regulation, and curcumin has demonstrated endothelial-protective and vasodilatory effects in clinical studies — however, patients should avoid commercial turmeric blends containing added salt or sodium-based anti-caking agents.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Curcumin's hypoglycaemic properties, high dietary fibre (22.7 g/100g), and negligible glycaemic load per culinary serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground turmeric is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; curcumin has been shown in multiple randomised controlled trials to improve insulin sensitivity, reduce HbA1c, and inhibit hepatic glucose production, while the glycaemic load per teaspoon serving is negligible (~0.3 GL units) — patients should avoid sweetened turmeric products such as golden milk mixes with added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Curcumin's anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties, low saturated fat (1.84 g/100g), high potassium (2080 mg/100g), and high dietary fibre (22.7 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground turmeric is beneficial for cardiovascular health; curcumin has demonstrated LDL-lowering, anti-platelet aggregation, and anti-inflammatory effects in clinical studies, saturated fat is low at 1.84 g/100g (contributing <0.06 g per teaspoon serving), and its high potassium and fibre content support vascular and lipid health — patients on anticoagulant therapy (e.g., warfarin) should use culinary quantities only and consult their physician before taking high-dose curcumin supplements.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Curcumin's direct inhibition of NF-κB, COX-2, TNF-alpha, and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground turmeric is the most evidence-based anti-inflammatory culinary spice for rheumatoid arthritis; curcumin directly inhibits NF-κB and COX-2 pathways and reduces TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines that drive joint inflammation — bioavailability is enhanced up to 2000% by co-consumption with black pepper (piperine), and patients on methotrexate or anticoagulants should restrict intake to culinary quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground turmeric is a finely milled bright yellow-orange powder derived from the dried rhizome of Curcuma longa, a plant native to South Asia and widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, India, and the tropics. It is one of the most widely used spices in the world, forming the backbone of South Asian, Southeast Asian, and Middle Eastern cuisines. The powder has a warm, earthy, mildly bitter, and slightly pungent flavour with a characteristic deep golden colour imparted by its primary bioactive compound, curcumin. In cooking, ground turmeric is used in curries, rice dishes (such as nasi kuning and biryani), soups, lentil dishes (dal), marinades, pickles, and golden milk (turmeric latte). It is a staple spice in Singaporean, Malaysian, Indian, Indonesian, and Sri Lankan cooking. Nutritionally, ground turmeric is remarkably dense per 100 g: it provides 312 kcal of energy, 9.68 g protein, 3.25 g fat (of which 1.84 g is saturated), 67.14 g carbohydrate, 22.7 g dietary fibre, 27 mg sodium, 2080 mg potassium, 168 mg calcium, and 55 mg iron. However, culinary serving sizes are small (approximately 1 teaspoon or 3 g), so the per-100g figures are not representative of actual intake. Clinically, curcumin — the principal polyphenol in turmeric — has been extensively studied for its anti-inflammatory, hypoglycaemic, lipid-modulating, and endothelial-protective properties. It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Ground turmeric is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is classified as a minimally processed spice with a low glycaemic index (~15) and negligible glycaemic load per serving.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of turmeric?
Is turmeric powder high in sodium?
Can I use turmeric if I have high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of curcumin?
How much turmeric should I use per serving?
Is ground turmeric the same as turmeric powder?
Is turmeric suitable for a vegan diet?
What is the potassium content of turmeric?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F10060036, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and select vitamins supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02043 (Spices, turmeric, ground), an approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis. Ontology codes are defensible and verified. All multilingual names are verified.
notes: 1. Core macros (protein_g 9.68, fat_g 3.25, carbohydrate_g 67.14) are all non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo; no zero-macro justification required. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB database; ground turmeric is a plant-derived spice and contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as turmeric is a plant-derived food with no animal-derived components. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 02043; plant-derived spice with no significant vitamin D. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 02043. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; minimally processed plant spice with no industrial trans fats. 2. Saturated fat (1.84 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.43 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.45 g/100g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C 25.9 mg, thiamine 0.15 mg, riboflavin 0.23 mg, niacin 5.14 mg, B6 1.80 mg, folate 39 mcg, vitamin E 3.10 mg, phosphorus 268 mg, magnesium 193 mg, zinc 4.35 mg, selenium 6.2 mcg, water 11.36 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02043 (Spices, turmeric, ground) as HPB does not report these fields. 3. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as ground turmeric is a spice, not a grain product; 'low_sodium' and 'high_fibre' are retained as accurate. 4. GI of ~15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground turmeric exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 5. Iron value of 55 mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects the HPB-reported value; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy reporting ~47.5 mg/100g for dried turmeric — the HPB figure is retained as the primary approved source. 6. Vitamin C value of 25.9 mg/100g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 02043. 7. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best matches; SNOMED CT closest_parent match documented with gap note — code 227493005 refers to turmeric as a substance with no specific ground/powdered form code in SNOMED CT hierarchy.
